text: The surgeon performs a robotic right inguinal hernia repair using the Intuitive XI robot. The camera port is placed at the umbilicus to enter the abdomen.

The surgeon enters the abdomen and places the camera port at the umbilicus. Robotic ports are positioned laterally on both sides of the abdomen, with the surgeon inserting the robotic trocar on one side and the assistant placing the contralateral trocar. The XI robot allows for close placement of ports, but they are positioned more laterally for cosmetic reasons, minimizing visible scars from the front.

The surgeon places robotic ports laterally in the abdomen on both the right and left sides, using the XI robot. The ports are positioned close together, with the scars located on the sides of the abdomen.

The robotic system is positioned over the camera port at the umbilicus using laser crosshairs for precise alignment. The scrub tech adjusts the height to prevent collisions, while the circulator moves the robotic boom into place, assisted by the surgeon.

The robotic system is moved into position, with the scrub tech adjusting the height to avoid collisions. The circulator aligns the robotic boom using laser crosshairs to position it directly over the camera port at the umbilicus, with the surgeon assisting in directing the setup.

The fourth arm of the XI robot is repositioned away from the surgical field as it is not needed for the hernia repair. The camera arm is then engaged, and the endoscope is installed. The robotic system guides the positioning of the endoscope to target the dissection site internally, adjusting the arms to optimize the view and access for the procedure.

The fourth arm of the XI robot is repositioned away from the surgical field as it is not needed for this operation. The camera arm is then engaged, and the endoscope is installed to begin targeting within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to internally target the site of dissection, adjusting the robotic arms into optimal positions to avoid collisions and effectively reach the target anatomy.

The robotic arms are positioned to avoid collisions and reach the target anatomy. Under direct visualization, the surgeon inserts the robotic scissors into the abdomen, ensuring the sharp tips do not cause injury. The docking process is completed within minutes.

The surgeon places the robotic scissor under direct visualization into the abdomen, ensuring the sharp tips are carefully monitored to prevent injury. Once the scissor is positioned, the docking of the robotic system is completed.

The surgeon begins the robotic right inguinal hernia repair by making a curvilinear incision anterior to the hernia defect using sharp scissors, cautery scissors, or hook cautery. The incision is extended medially to the median umbilical ligament, marking the medial border of the dissection.

The surgeon begins the operation at the console by making a curvilinear incision anterior to the defect. The incision is made using sharp scissors, cautery scissors, or hook cautery, extending medially to the median umbilical ligament, marking the medial border of the dissection.

The surgeon begins dissection in the preperitoneal space, working straight down the median umbilical ligament to identify the pubic tubercle. Blunt dissection is used to safely separate tissue without risking injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon begins the dissection by working straight down the median umbilical ligament, using blunt dissection to safely identify the pubic tubercle without risking injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a transabdominal approach to visualize the spermatic cord and surrounding vascular structures. The peritoneum is lifted to reveal the cord structures, with the indirect defect visible lateral to the cord. The anatomy is clearly identified during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies a firm structure, with vascular structures immediately lateral to it. The spermatic cord is positioned anterior and lateral to the vascular structures, while the indirect defect is lateral to the cord. Using the transabdominal approach, the surgeon lifts the peritoneum to visualize the cord structures clearly and continues the dissection.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal hernia sac free from the spermatic cord structures using primarily blunt dissection, with occasional sharp dissection or cautery. The sac is carefully separated from the cord structures to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal hernia sac free from the cord structures using primarily blunt dissection, with occasional sharp dissection or cautery. The sac is carefully separated and retracted back onto the cord structures.

The surgeon inserts the pro grip anatomic mesh, which adheres to the posterior aspect of the muscle due to its velcro-like backing. The mesh is secured using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture, with barbs ensuring secure placement. The surgeon runs additional stitches up the medial aspect, pulling on the suture to secure it to Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon inserts the ProGrip anatomic mesh with a Velcro-like backing, securing it to the posterior aspect of the muscle without sutures. The robotic XI instrumentation is used to suture the mesh with a 2-0 absorbable V-Lock suture, featuring barbs for secure placement and a loop for knotless initial throws. Additional stitches are run up the medial aspect, ensuring a secure bite of Cooper's ligament by pulling on the suture.
The surgeon places interrupted sutures along the conjoined tendon or around the superior edge of the mesh, ensuring the mesh is laid out flat against the muscle tissue.

The surgeon runs a suture along the conjoined tendon and around the superior edge of the mesh, ensuring it is laid flat. The final step involves closing the peritoneum using a three-oh V-lock suture with wristed instruments from the XI robot, providing a continuous closure to prevent internal hernias. The suture line is completed without a knot by backing up a few throws to lock the suture, marking the end of the operation.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments from the XI robot to close the peritoneum with a continuous three-oh V-lock suture, ensuring a secure closure that prevents gapping and potential internal hernia formation.
The surgeon completes the suture line using a V-lock suture, backing up a few throws to lock the suture without a knot. The peritoneum is closed, marking the end of the operation.
